metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `.github/workflows/release.yml` as an unenumerated consumer of the tag lock and specify how version tags will be created post-cutover (e.g., tag via Gerrit submit strategy, add a CI service-account bypass, or document a manual deploy-key-signed tag push procedure).
findings.finding: The tag lock ruleset (gerrit-mirror-lock-tags) rejects all tag creation/update by non-bypass actors, but .github/workflows/release.yml publishes to PyPI on `push: tags: ['v*']` — a human operator git-push of a version tag. After cutover, that tag push will be rejected by the ruleset, silently breaking the PyPI release workflow. The plan enumerates no strategy for creating release tags post-cutover (e.g., pushing tags via Gerrit, adding a CI bypass, or a separate deploy-key-tagged push).
findings.location: Scope section + What §1 ('activate the Gerrit-only ruleset') / .github/workflows/release.yml lines 18-20
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section to the cutover plan noting that `release.yml`'s `push: tags: v*` trigger requires a human or CI tag push that will now be blocked. Options: (a) add the CI GITHUB_TOKEN actor as a bypass_actor for tags only, (b) create tags exclusively through a Gerrit submit strategy, or (c) tag via a one-off deploy-key-authenticated push. Document the chosen path in CONTRIBUTING.md and/or the runbook.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a post-rollback Terraform reconciliation step to the runbook: after any raw-API ruleset deletion, run `terraform state rm <ruleset_resource>` (or `terraform apply -target`) so IaC state matches GitHub reality.
findings.finding: The raw-API rollback path (`gh api -X DELETE` or temporary bypass_actor) bypasses Terraform, introducing state drift between GitHub's actual ruleset state and the S6 Terraform state file; no `terraform state` reconciliation step is specified.
findings.location: ## Rollback / emergency restore — Fast un-lock bullet
findings.suggested_fix: In the runbook Fast un-lock section, add: 'If using `gh api -X DELETE` (not `terraform destroy`): immediately run `terraform state rm module.github_repo.github_repository_ruleset.gerrit_only` to prevent drift; schedule a `terraform plan` check within 24h to confirm no unintended re-apply.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a hard precondition check to the live-apply AC: verify that WS6/LLM-Review is actively voting on a test CL before the cutover apply proceeds, so a broken bot cannot silently freeze main.
findings.finding: The cutover is a single one-shot flip with no hard gate ensuring WS6 (LLM-Review bot) is operational before main is locked; if the bot is misconfigured or down at cutover time, all Gerrit submissions will stall and main will be frozen with no human merge path.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC line under Live-gated: '[ ] A test Gerrit CL receives a WS6 LLM-Review vote (pass or fail) before the cutover apply step — cutover is gated on confirmed bot liveness.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit pre-flight check to the live cutover procedure: verify the LLM-Review bot is operational (e.g., confirm `infra/gerrit/reviewbot-e2e.sh` passes OR the CloudWatch S4b alarm is in OK state) before activating the DeployKey-only lock on GitHub main.
findings.finding: The plan's WS6 dependency is stated as a soft 'relates_to' note ('the review-bot must be voting before Gerrit-only enforcement is meaningful') without a hard pre-flight gate. If WS7's cutover executes while the LLM-Review bot (WS6) is down or misconfigured, every Gerrit change will be permanently stuck at `0` LLM-Review — unsubmittable — and no change can reach GitHub main. The plan has no explicit pre-cutover check that the bot is operational.
findings.location: Plan section 'Dependencies'; plan section 'Live cutover apply + proof' (Acceptance Criteria, live-gated)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a post-cutover observability requirement: specify at minimum (a) an alert or periodic check that GitHub main SHA matches the latest Gerrit-replicated commit (replication health), and (b) a Terraform drift-detection run or GitHub audit-log alert if the ruleset configuration changes unexpectedly.
findings.finding: No post-cutover observability is specified for the two new failure modes introduced: (1) Gerrit replication silently failing (main stops advancing), and (2) ruleset state drifting back to unprotected (e.g., via manual GitHub UI change).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an automated, recurring check (e.g. a scheduled GitHub Actions workflow or a CI job) that verifies the `gerrit-mirror-lock-main` and `gerrit-mirror-lock-tags` rulesets are active on `navapbc/rebar`, and alerts (or fails loudly) if either is absent or disabled.
findings.finding: No automated alerting or monitoring is defined for unexpected out-of-band mutation or deletion of the Gerrit-only mirror-lock rulesets. If an admin removes the lock manually (drift), the only detection method described is a manual poll (`gh api /repos/navapbc/rebar/rulesets ...`), leaving the team blind to a security-critical regression.
findings.location: Observability & Ownership (T10g) — plan section 'Live cutover apply + proof'; infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md § Verify empirically
findings.suggested_fix: Add a lightweight scheduled GitHub Actions workflow (e.g. daily, on `workflow_dispatch`) that runs `gh api /repos/navapbc/rebar/rulesets --jq '.[] | select(.name | startswith("gerrit-mirror-lock")) | {name, enforcement}'` and fails/notifies if the count is not 2 or any enforcement is not `active`. This provides automated drift detection without new infrastructure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the `check` block in `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` with a `lifecycle { precondition { ... } }` block on the `github_repository_ruleset.main_lock` resource so that the deploy-key absence actually aborts `terraform apply`, not just emits a warning.
findings.finding: The plan (and `main.tf` comment) claims the Terraform `check "deploy_key_present"` block makes the apply 'fail loudly' if the S5 replication deploy key is absent — but Terraform `check` blocks (introduced in v1.5) emit a WARNING and do NOT abort `terraform apply`. The Path A (Terraform) cutover can therefore complete and activate the DeployKey-only lock even when the deploy key is missing, locking out replication along with everyone else.
findings.location: infra/terraform-github/main.tf lines 49–62 (`check "deploy_key_present"` block); plan section 'Context — CONSUMES d251/S6' and runbook 'Path A — Terraform'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the dangling 'S7 runbook' reference in the draft CONTRIBUTING.md content (github-mirror-lock.md line 198): either (a) add authoring a contributor-facing recovery runbook to WS7's scope, (b) redirect to the existing `infra/runbooks/review-bot-ops.md` §'Manually re-run a review', or (c) inline the /rerun recovery steps directly into CONTRIBUTING.md.
findings.finding: The draft CONTRIBUTING.md content (embedded in infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md §'Contributor workflow under mirror mode') directs contributors to 'ADR-0009 / S7 runbook' for /rerun recovery, but no 'S7 runbook' exists anywhere in infra/runbooks/ — the reference is dangling. WS7 is the ticket that would produce CONTRIBUTING.md, yet the plan does not specify authoring this runbook, leaving the /rerun recovery fallback under-specified for contributors who hit a stuck -1.
findings.location: infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md, line 198 (§'Contributor workflow under mirror mode / CONTRIBUTING.md content'); Acceptance Criteria §'Doable now', CONTRIBUTING.md AC bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'see ADR-0009 / S7 runbook' with 'see [infra/runbooks/review-bot-ops.md](infra/runbooks/review-bot-ops.md) §"Manually re-run a review"' — that runbook already documents the /rerun endpoint, auth, and semantics for exactly this scenario, and is an existing WS7-consumable artifact.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'WS7 verifies that runbook's rollback steps are current' with a concrete check: specify that the runbook's ruleset ID, API endpoint, and bypass-actor commands must be diffed against the final S6 Terraform outputs and confirmed to match before the live apply.
findings.finding: 'WS7 verifies that runbook's rollback steps are current for the final ruleset' defers a judgment call to the executor without specifying what criteria make the runbook 'current' or how the verification would produce evidence.
findings.location: Rollback / emergency restore section — 'WS7 verifies that runbook's rollback steps are current for the final ruleset and links them from CONTRIBUTING.md'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the rollback section: 'Currency check: confirm that the ruleset ID in the runbook matches `terraform output gerrit_ruleset_id` (or the equivalent S6 output), and that the `gh api -X DELETE` endpoint in the runbook matches the live ruleset URL returned by `gh api /repos/navapbc/rebar/rulesets`; record the confirmed IDs in the ticket.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the untraceable 'WS6-b's live-env boundary' reference with a citable precedent (e.g., a specific ADR, runbook section, or ticket id that documents the access-boundary pattern) or remove the analogy and state the rationale directly.
findings.finding: The plan references 'WS6-b's live-env boundary' as a precedent for its own access-boundary treatment, but no file, runbook, ADR, or ticket labeled 'WS6-b' exists anywhere in the codebase. This is an unverifiable assertion used to justify a scoping decision.
findings.location: Boundary section: 'analogous to WS6-b's live-env boundary'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'analogous to WS6-b's live-env boundary' with a direct statement such as 'consistent with the access-boundary pattern documented in `infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md` (S6 apply-prove-rollback scope)' — which is the actual proven precedent in the codebase.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `.github/workflows/test.yml`, `optionality.yml`, and `eval.yml` as consumers of the cutover and explicitly state whether their `pull_request: {}` triggers should be removed, left as dead code for feature-branch use, or converted to Gerrit-aware triggers.
findings.finding: The GitHub Actions CI workflows (test.yml, optionality.yml, eval.yml) include `pull_request: {}` as a trigger. After the Gerrit-only cutover, no PR can ever be opened/merged against `main`, making this trigger permanently dead for the main branch. The plan does not enumerate these workflows as consumers to audit or acknowledge, so it is unclear whether the `pull_request` trigger should be removed, left as dead code, or intentionally retained for feature branches.
findings.location: What §1 (cutover activation) / .github/workflows/test.yml line 19, optionality.yml line 18, eval.yml line 21

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the CONTRIBUTING.md AC (and the doc itself) the non-happy-path scenario where a contributor pushes without the Change-Id hook installed: what error Gerrit returns, how to install the hook retroactively, and how to amend the commit and re-push.
findings.finding: The CONTRIBUTING.md acceptance criterion does not explicitly cover the 'missing Change-Id hook' non-happy path — one of the most common contributor stumbles — nor does it specify what error a developer sees and what remediation step to take (install the hook, amend the commit, re-push).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Doable now' first bullet (CONTRIBUTING.md AC)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the CONTRIBUTING.md section to include: (1) the exact Gerrit error when Change-Id is missing ('missing Change-Id in commit message footer'), (2) the hook install command (`scp -p -P 29418 <user>@<gerrit-host>:hooks/commit-msg .git/hooks/` or `curl` equivalent), (3) the `git commit --amend --no-edit && git push origin HEAD:refs/for/main` recovery sequence.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command to the Terraform-reconcile AC, e.g., '`terraform plan` must output "No changes" (exit 0) against the target workspace, OR `gh api /repos/navapbc/rebar/rulesets` must list exactly the S6 ruleset and no legacy `main-protection` id 18048287'.
findings.finding: The 'Doable now' AC that requires the cutover plan to be 'reconciled against the S6 Terraform (no drift / no competing ruleset)' names the desired outcome but provides no concrete proving command to produce evidence of zero drift.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria / Doable now — second bullet ('The cutover plan names the exact legacy ruleset(s)...; reconciled against the S6 Terraform (no drift / no competing ruleset)')
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the second 'Doable now' AC: 'Verified by running `terraform plan` (expects "No changes") and by `gh api /repos/navapbc/rebar/rulesets` returning exactly one active ruleset for `main` (the S6 DeployKey-only ruleset, not id 18048287).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pre-cutover step to the live-apply procedure that checks for open PRs targeting `main`, communicates the cutover to affected authors, and documents the migration path (e.g. convert the branch to a Gerrit change or rebase/close the PR before the ruleset is activated).
findings.finding: The cutover plan has no documented procedure for draining or communicating about in-flight GitHub PRs open against `main` at the moment the DeployKey-only ruleset is activated. Those PRs will be permanently unable to merge — they are not covered by either the fast-unlock rollback (which targets Gerrit unavailability, not stranded PRs) or the snapshot restore path.
findings.location: Rollback / emergency restore section; What #1; Acceptance Criteria (Live-gated AC #1)
findings.suggested_fix: Before activating the ruleset, run `gh pr list --base main --state open` and for each open PR: (1) notify the author that GitHub PRs are being disabled, (2) document how to re-submit via Gerrit (`git push gerrit HEAD:refs/for/main`), and (3) close the PR with a comment linking to CONTRIBUTING.md. Add this as a checklist item in the cutover runbook `infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit step to audit and update (or remove) any existing README sections and other docs that describe GitHub PRs as a valid contribution path, replacing or cross-referencing the new Gerrit flow documented in CONTRIBUTING.md.
findings.finding: The plan does not explicitly audit or update existing README body content (or other docs) that may currently describe GitHub PRs as the contribution path. Since the plan acknowledges 'devs still open GitHub PRs' today, other existing documentation likely references the PR workflow; those references become stale/misleading after cutover but the plan only adds a link to CONTRIBUTING.md from README rather than auditing and removing/replacing stale PR-workflow text.
findings.location: 'What' section item 2 / Acceptance Criteria: 'linked from README'
findings.suggested_fix: Before or alongside the CONTRIBUTING.md PR, grep the repo for 'pull request', 'open a PR', 'github.com/.*/pull' patterns in markdown docs and update them to say 'GitHub is a read-only mirror — see CONTRIBUTING.md for the Gerrit-based contribution flow.' Explicitly list this audit as an AC item under 'Doable now'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact operation sequence in the cutover runbook: (1) verify S6 Gerrit-only ruleset is active and enforcing, THEN (2) delete legacy ruleset 18048287 — never delete first — and add an API call to confirm both rulesets' states before and after.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no sequencing or atomicity guarantee for the delete-old-ruleset → confirm-new-ruleset-active transition; a failure between these two steps leaves main temporarily unprotected.
findings.location: ## What — item 1 / Rollback / emergency restore section
findings.suggested_fix: In the runbook/AC, add: 'Step 1: confirm S6 ruleset is active (`gh api /repos/navapbc/rebar/rulesets/{s6_id}`). Step 2: test rejection (push attempt). Step 3 only then: delete legacy ruleset 18048287. Step 4: re-confirm only S6 ruleset remains.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `README.md` explicitly to the Scope IN list as an edit target (adding a link to CONTRIBUTING.md), so it is not omitted during implementation.
findings.finding: The plan says CONTRIBUTING.md will be 'linked from README' (AC, 'Doable now' bullet 1), but README.md is not listed as an explicit edit target in the Scope section. If the implementer reads Scope as the authoritative list of files to touch, README.md may be skipped — leaving CONTRIBUTING.md unlinked from the repo root document.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, change 'IN: ... the CONTRIBUTING.md contributor guide' to 'IN: ... the CONTRIBUTING.md contributor guide; a README.md link to CONTRIBUTING.md'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise the cutover sequencing to create-then-delete: confirm the new DeployKey-only rulesets are `active` (via `gh api /repos/navapbc/rebar/rulesets`) BEFORE removing the legacy `main-protection` ruleset (id 18048287), eliminating the unprotected window.
findings.finding: The `apply-mirror-lock.sh` script deletes the legacy `main-protection` ruleset (step 1) BEFORE creating the new DeployKey-only lock rulesets (steps 2–3). During the window between those two operations, `main` is entirely unprotected — neither the old nor the new ruleset is active. For a permanent cutover, the safe ordering is: create new locks first, verify they are active, THEN remove the legacy protection.
findings.location: infra/github/apply-mirror-lock.sh lines 67–71 (delete step 1) vs. lines 93–147 (create steps 2–3); plan section 'What' item 1 and 'Live cutover apply + proof'
findings.suggested_fix: In `apply-mirror-lock.sh`, move the `--delete-main-protection` deletion to a final step (after the new locks are created and their IDs are echoed), so the sequence is: (1) verify deploy key, (2) create new locks, (3) optionally delete old protection. Add a confirmation prompt or explicit `gh api` read-back before the delete.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `backend {}` block to `infra/terraform-github/` (or a `backend.tf`) pointing to the project's encrypted remote state (e.g. S3 with SSE), or explicitly gate the env-var-only path in CI so `var.github_token` is never passable in the live apply workflow.
findings.finding: The Terraform IaC for the mirror-lock has no declared backend configuration: no `backend {}` block appears in main.tf and no backend.tf is present in infra/terraform-github/. If an operator runs `terraform apply` with local state (the default), any value passed for `var.github_token` lands in plaintext terraform.tfstate — even though the variable is marked `sensitive = true`, Terraform's sensitive flag only suppresses CLI display, not state-file encryption.
findings.location: infra/terraform-github/main.tf — no backend block; infra/terraform-github/ directory has no backend.tf
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `backend "s3" {}` block (or equivalent) to infra/terraform-github/ with server-side encryption enabled, consistent with the runbook's reference to 'rebar/prod/github.tfstate from the AWS stack'. Alternatively, remove the `github_token` variable entirely and enforce that the provider only reads GITHUB_TOKEN from the environment, eliminating the tfvars path.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
